class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eric Slaughter, former commissioner of the Housing Authority of Cook County, participates in the housing authority’s board meeting via Zoom.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“They need to bring on new people so rather than me being in the way, let somebody else deal with it,” Slaughter said. “If you want my expertise, I will give it to you. If you don’t want it, I will move on.”</p><p>He declined to comment on if his departure had to do with an investigation last year into his conduct.</p><p>Soler and Salleh-Barone didn’t respond to requests for comment.</p><p>The investigation into Slaughter followed complaints from the housing authority’s head of human resources, who said Slaughter created a “toxic and hostile environment” and took “direct shots” at her and other women in senior leadership. Slaughter had been vocal at meetings, raising questions about the agency’s spending.</p><p>The investigator determined Slaughter’s conduct was “unintentional sexual harassment and discriminatory” and his conduct puts the agency at “risk of legal liability.” The Sun-Times previously reported that some commissioners had concerns about the attorney who was hired to conduct the investigation.</p><p>The new commissioners all have experience in the real estate industry.</p><p>Schiff is a former president of the Illinois Housing Council, according to the organization’s website. He has a background in developing and financing affordable housing, including partnerships with public housing authorities, according to the news release.</p><p>Little is a development finance consultant for the Chicago TREND Corp., a group focused on commercial development in low- and moderate-income neighborhoods, according to its website. Previously, he was an executive in the real estate and community development space, working on both public and private initiatives to expand affordable housing, the news release said.</p><p>Johnson-Black has worked for the city of Chicago and agencies such as HUD and the Cook County Recorder of Deeds, according to the news release.</p><p>Johnson-Black and Little have worked for the Chicago Housing Authority.</p><p>“Our new commissioners will help guide our Culture of Excellence as we continue improving performance, strengthening partnerships, and enhancing service to residents throughout suburban Cook County,” Housing Authority of Cook County Executive Director Danita Childers said in the news release. “This is another important step toward our goal of becoming a high-performing agency.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/01/cook-county-housing-authority-childers-turmoil-firings-investigation-spending" >Cook County housing authority in turmoil amid firings, investigation, spending concerns</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Justin Ishbia's Shore Capital Partners to buy South Loop land that could be used for new White Sox stadium
entries.summary: <p>The White Sox may have taken the first step to securing land that could be home to a new stadium.</p><p>On Wednesday, owner-in-waiting Justin Ishbia’s private equity firm, Shore Capital Partners, was in the process of buying a 47-acre rail yard in the South Loop. Justin's brother, Mat, owns the NBA's Phoenix Suns.</p><p>The piece of land is located across the Chicago River from The 78, where the Sun-Times reported the Sox were considering building a new stadium in 2024. Crain’s Chicago was first to report on Ishbia’s firm buying the rail yard in the South Loop.</p><p>The rail yard could also be used for a new health care facility. </p><p>The Chicago Fire recently broke ground on a $750 million privately funded stadium in early March. There have been discussions about the Sox building a new stadium on that land, but there are concerns that the The 78 would be too narrow for a baseball stadium.</p><p>The Sox’ lease at Rate Field runs through 2029. Ishbia has the option to purchase controlling interest in the Sox from owner Jerry Reinsdorf from 2029-33.</p><p>“Our White Sox focus has been and continues to be solely on the potential of a new ballpark at The 78," the team said in a statement. </p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: LB Jack Sanborn rejoins Bears after year with Cowboys
entries.summary: <p>One year after Jack Sanborn followed Matt Eberflus to the Cowboys, the linebacker returned to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> on a one-year deal Wednesday. </p><p>Sanborn, who turns 26 in July, went to Lake Zurich High School. The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/18/bears-sign-james-lynch-one-year-deal-free-agency-titans-vikings-defensive-tackle-depth-nfl" target="_blank" >Bears</a> signed him as an undrafted free agent out of Wisconsin and started him 19 times from 2022-24.</p><p>In Dallas, Sanborn played for Eberflus, who was named their defensive coordinator after the Bears fired him in 2024, and linebackers coach Dave Borgonzi. Sanborn played just six games because of a concussion and groin injury.</p><p>Sanborn will provide depth at an injury-ravaged linebacker spot. The Bears are awaiting the return of T.J. Edwards from a leg injury and Noah Sewell from a torn Achilles’ tendon. The Bears signed Devin Bush last week to replace Tremaine Edmunds, whom they released as a cap casualty. Sanborn is expected to help on special teams, too.<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Illinois Democrats ran on anti-Trump messaging. Now what?
entries.summary: <p>It's over. Now what?</p><p>That’s the question for all of the Illinois Democrats who sold themselves as fierce fighters against President Donald Trump.</p><p>Illinois being heavy Democratic turf, the nominees minted in the Tuesday primary will likely clinch their elections this November.</p><p>Chicago-area voters have been bombarded with broadcast and digital ads for weeks now with this anti-Trump messaging — for Cook County, state, Congress and Senate offices. In simple terms, positive ads cast candidates as anti-Trump, anti-ICE superfighters, while a firehose of negative ads painted rivals as duplicitous MAGA operatives.</p><p>Tens of millions of dollars from pro-cryptocurrency and pro-AI groups and political networks connected to the American Israel Public Affairs Committee fueled the unrelenting Democratic primary ad blitz. These special interest groups never revealed their true agendas and we are not better off as a community to be subjected to this political trickery.</p><p>We can however, look at what this may mean for Democrats leading up to the general election in November. </p><h3>Opposition to Trump fired up Illinois Democrats</h3><p>Illinois has one of the earliest primaries in the country, following the March 3 primary with Arkansas, North Carolina and Texas. Mississippi was on March 10. Illinois, however, is the first big blue state for Democrats to test anti-Trump political messaging ahead of the midterm elections.</p><p>That’s why these primaries never paid much attention to very local, unique issues. In this sense the Illinois contests were nationalized because given the dire moment we are in, opposition to Trump <i>was</i> the local issue.</p><p>And look at what our community has been through since Trump regained the White House last year: ICE and Border Patrol raids; snatching people off the street; terrorizing communities and deporting people without proper due process. Plus, slashes in federal funding for all sorts of programs to punish Illinois for being a blue state that values health care, immigrants, food assistance, diversity and all kinds of rights. On top of that, tariffs impacting local businesses and your pocketbook. And much, much more.</p><p>It's no wonder voters told us on Tuesday that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/live/election-day-illinois-primaries-2026-results-analysis#0000019c-fcca-d6fc-a3fe-fddb0e980000" target="_blank" >immigration was a top reason</a> for showing up at the polls. </p><p></p><h3>Reality check: Democrats have little power</h3><p>To the new nominees: Details, please, on how to win this uphill battle to save our democracy. That is, suggest something Democrats are not doing already. Because all I’ve heard in ads for you are screams and theatrics about fighting Trump.</p><p>There are plenty of Democrats in local, state, and federal governments already battling Trump. The reality is, if these experienced Democrats already in office could stop Trump, they would have.</p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker, renominated and poised to win another term, said Tuesday night he has been fighting Trump for years. “Friends, this is the fight of our lives," he said. "Everything we care about is under siege from Washington."</p><p>It’s not for lack of will. Every Democrat opposes Trump and wants to fight. Unless Democrats flip the House or Senate this November, with little power, it's hard to find the way.</p><p>As for the next peoples' fight against Trump, a "No Kings” demonstration at Butler Field in Grant Park is scheduled for March 28 in the Chicago suburbs and across the nation.</p>
entries.author: Lynn Sweet

entries.title: Blackhawks' current usage of Nick Lardis isn't working and doesn't make sense
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> called up rookie forward Nick Lardis after the trade deadline only to bury him in a fourth-line role, giving him less than 11 minutes of ice time per game.</p><p>The usage doesn't make sense.</p><p>Coach Jeff Blashill gave an interesting explanation Monday that it's easier for a player to establish himself in the NHL if he shows he can fit in all 12 forward spots, not just in the top six.</p><p>Lardis demonstrating that "he can add offense without having to be on the ice with the very best players on the team" would theoretically double his job prospects, Blashill said. He added that it's "hard to learn how to play 10 minutes a night when you’re playing 20 minutes a night."</p><p>All of that may be true. But it's not depth forwards whom the Hawks lack. They have plenty of those, and will likely find plenty more within their enormous prospect pool.</p><p>The biggest thing actually holding the Hawks back right now is a lack of top-six forwards. On the current roster, they have approximately three legitimate top-sixers: Connor Bedard, Frank Nazar and Tyler Bertuzzi. </p><p>They're seriously counting on top prospects Anton Frondell and Roman Kantserov both hitting, but that's not guaranteed, as promising as they appear to be.</p><p>Right now, before Frondell and Kantserov's arrivals, why not try Lardis in a top-six role? This is the time to take risks, and the 20-year-old clearly has talent. He didn't rack up 117 points in the OHL last season and 32 points in 35 games in the AHL this season without it.</p><p>Plus, from the Hawks' perspective, given how many lottery tickets they own, a prospect with a 30% chance of becoming a top-six forward and a 70% chance of busting is probably more valuable than one with a 70% chance of becoming a bottom-six forward and a 30% chance of busting.</p><p>On defense, Blashill has actually embraced the importance of giving youngsters ample opportunity. On Tuesday against the Wild, he went as far as healthy-scratching steady veteran Matt Grzelcyk to get Ethan Del Mastro in the lineup.</p><p>It's perplexing how unwilling he is to follow the same approach with forwards — particularly with veteran Andre Burakovsky, who's showing no signs of breaking out of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/02/blackhawks-andre-burakovsky-jeff-blashill-trust-confidence-connor-bedard-line" target="_blank" >his slump</a>.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/02/blackhawks-andre-burakovsky-jeff-blashill-trust-confidence-connor-bedard-line" >Blackhawks’ Andre Burakovsky thankful for Jeff Blashill’s trust as he searches for confidence</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Blashill mentioned he has double-shifted Bedard in Sam Lafferty's fourth-line center spot occasionally, giving Lardis a little time with the Hawks' star.</p><p>However, Lardis simply isn't getting enough shifts in general — only 12 all game Tuesday, for example. And he's still spending most of his time with Lafferty and Landon Slaggert, who aren't exactly offensive facilitators.</p><p>It's not working well, either. The Hawks have been outscored 2-0 and outshot 21-8 — with an abysmal 8.8% expected-goals ratio — during Lardis' five-on-five ice time in five games since his call-up. Individually, he has just one shot (and it came on the power play).</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Nick Lardis with a one-timer that's stopped by Hill <a href="https://t.co/JomHdU2Vnx">pic.twitter.com/JomHdU2Vnx</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2033039063062560949?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 15, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Compare that to Lardis' stats in 21 games in December and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/10/blackhawks-nick-lardis-settling-nhl-oliver-moore-friendship-shootouts" target="_blank" >January</a>, largely while Bedard was injured: 13:39 of ice time per game, 32 shots on goal, five goals, 46.4% expected-goals ratio.</p><p>Another comparison: Predators forward Luke Evangelista, a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/12/21/blackhawks-nick-lardis-nhl-future-predators-luke-evangelista-consistency" target="_blank" >similar player to Lardis in many ways</a>, averaged a sizable 16:34 of ice time when called up for the final 24 games of the 2022-23 season.</p><p>Evangelista tallied seven goals and eight assists during that trial run, earning a permanent NHL job, and he's now pacing for nearly 60 points in his fourth season in Nashville.</p><p>Could the Hawks have a similar gem in Lardis? They'll only find out if they give him an opportunity to prove it.</p><p>"When I do get my opportunity, [I want to make sure] I'm taking full advantage of it and showing these little details to the coaches so they can trust me with more minutes," Lardis said Tuesday.</p><h3><b>Little details</b></h3><p>To give the Hawks some credit, their staff is working diligently with Lardis to improve his skills and make him a more well-rounded player, despite his lack of in-game opportunities.</p><p>Blashill mentioned Lardis "has a natural tendency to not want to skate with the puck" that the Hawks are trying to change. Lardis agreed with that.</p><p>"When you get the puck as a younger guy, I don't want to say you panic, but you sometimes want to give it up right away," Lardis said.</p><p>"[It's] about wanting the puck more on the breakout, skating with the puck and not deferring to just making a play standing still. It's something I've got to keep continuing to get better at. There's been times where I feel like I've done it and...opened space for myself and my teammates to make an easy play. And there's been times where I haven't, and I've seen how it makes a difference."</p><p>During practices and video sessions, he's working on memorizing teammates' assigned routes on defensive-zone breakouts so he will instinctively know where they're going to be. That's something more NHL experience should help with.</p><p>He's also working on quick scans of the ice before he gets the puck. He appeared to do that during his last shift of the second period Tuesday, for example, and it allowed him and Lafferty to execute a smooth breakout.</p><p>"You have that awareness after a good scan to skate the puck, get out of the zone," Lardis said. "You're not just deferring to chipping it out or making a pass back to your 'D.'"</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: College student from Elmhurst disappears during spring break trip to Spain
entries.summary: <p>An Elmhurst family is seeking help finding their son, who disappeared Tuesday in Barcelona, Spain.</p><p>James “Jimmy” Gracey, 20, disappeared from the <a class="Link" href="https://shoko.biz/" target="_blank" >Shoko nightclub</a> around 3 a.m.</p><p>He was on a spring break trip to visit friends studying in Barcelona.</p><p>Gracey attends the University of Alabama.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260318/news/elmhurst-man-disappears-on-vacation-in-spain/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a></p>
entries.author: Susan Sarkauskas | Daily Herald

entries.title: To pay for tourism boost, Chicago ramps up hotel tax, making it nation's highest
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s already sky-high hotel tax will climb to 19%, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/27/finance-committee-hotel-tax-hike-chicago-tourism-budget" >highest in the nation,</a> to help market the city to convention organizers and tourists.</p><p>The City Council made it happen on Wednesday by unanimously agreeing to create a Tourism Improvement District and raise the tax on hotel rooms within that district to 19%. The current combined city, county and state tax on hotel rooms is 17.5%.</p><p>Alderpersons who normally wince at raising taxes jumped at the chance to boost the marketing budget for the convention and tourism agency known as Choose Chicago.</p><p>South Side Ald. David Moore (17th) jumped on board after voting no in committee to protest the fact that the 1.5 percentage point increase would also be paid by Chicago residents on staycations.</p><p>Hotels in all or part of 14 different wards — including Downtown, McCormick Place, the Illinois Medical District and Hyde Park — have volunteered to impose the increase for the next five years. </p><p>The tax is expected to generate more than $50 million a year to sell Chicago, and double a marketing budget that pales in comparison to other cities. </p><p>The Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority has an annual operating budget of $457 million, according to a comparison prepared by Choose Chicago. That’s followed by Visit Orlando ($116 million), Discover Los Angeles ($62 million), the San Diego Tourism Authority ($56.9 million) and New York’s NYC & Company ($45 million).</p><p>The increase would apply to room rates at hotels with 100 or more rooms that agree to opt in. Revenue would be used to market the city, bankroll incentives and cover bid fees, such as the $1 million required to enter the competition to host the Democratic National Convention. </p><p>Choose Chicago CEO Kristen Reynolds has argued that Chicago's convention competitors "know very well that Chicago is underfunded, and they use it to their advantage" to attract the same conventions, visitors and events Chicago is eyeing, but also to "lure away legacy clients that have been coming to Chicago for decades."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" alt="Alderpeople attend a Chicago City Council meeting at City Hall on Wednesday." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc4ca01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5657x3175+0+298/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F50%2F1aaf01eb486a8a53a4da971299b0%2Fcouncil-260319-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/41c9255/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5657x3175+0+298/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F50%2F1aaf01eb486a8a53a4da971299b0%2Fcouncil-260319-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc4ca01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5657x3175+0+298/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F50%2F1aaf01eb486a8a53a4da971299b0%2Fcouncil-260319-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Alderpeople attend a Chicago City Council meeting at City Hall on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Also on Wednesday, Johnson handed the permanent job to acting Cultural Affairs and Special Events Commissioner Kenya Merritt, while the City Council confirmed his choices of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/13/copa-chief-lakenya-white-chicago-police-department-oversight" >LaKenya White</a> to lead the Civilian Office of Police Accountability and<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/10/susan-cappello-chicago-animal-care-and-control-chief-council-committee-approval" > Susan Cappello</a> to be the permanent executive director of the Commission on Animal Care and Control.</p><p>More than two dozen animal welfare organizations and scores of shelter volunteers have condemned Cappello’s leadership and demanded that the Johnson administration conduct a nationwide search for her replacement.</p><p>They have described a toxic environment of “hostility, manipulation, favoritism and retaliation” where volunteers who complain are summarily dismissed. They have raised concerns about overcrowded and unsanitary shelter conditions, where dogs are held in cages, sometimes in their own waste — and often walked just once a week.</p><p>PAWS Chicago and other organizations and advocates have countered that Cappello is being “asked to do the impossible” in a city that earmarks just $8 million a year for animal care and control, less than one-third of the average spent in other major cities.</p><p>The spending shortage comes at a time when the number of dogs and cats arriving at the city-run shelter has surged by nearly 5,000 over the last two years. That’s because Chicagoans who can no longer afford to support themselves are choosing to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/07/25/pet-owner-surrender-dogs-cats-overwhelming-shelters-rescues" >relinquish their pets </a>to a shelter that turns no animal away.</p><p>Animal Care and Control leadership has long been a revolving door, with a steady parade of executive directors struggling to solve the same chronic issues.</p><p>"This is a tough job, people. We don't give them enough money," Northwest Side Ald. Nick Sposato (38th) said Wednesday.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-120003" name="image-120003"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d86b254/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5002x2807+0+264/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fcf%2Fd2d6f03e4817b169191b5b5c9d54%2Fcouncil-260319-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04a526d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5002x2807+0+264/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fcf%2Fd2d6f03e4817b169191b5b5c9d54%2Fcouncil-260319-05.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a3ffaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5002x2807+0+264/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fcf%2Fd2d6f03e4817b169191b5b5c9d54%2Fcouncil-260319-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ald. Ray Lopez (15th) speaks Wednesday during a Chicago City Council meeting." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a3ffaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5002x2807+0+264/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fcf%2Fd2d6f03e4817b169191b5b5c9d54%2Fcouncil-260319-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8cbfddb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5002x2807+0+264/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fcf%2Fd2d6f03e4817b169191b5b5c9d54%2Fcouncil-260319-05.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a3ffaf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5002x2807+0+264/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Fcf%2Fd2d6f03e4817b169191b5b5c9d54%2Fcouncil-260319-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ald. Ray Lopez (15th) speaks Wednesday during a Chicago City Council meeting.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>After confirming White, the Council approved a stalled measure authorizing COPA to investigate allegations against police officers accused of violating the city’s Welcoming City ordinance. Ald. Ray Lopez (15th) argued that the ordinance contradicts Johnson’s executive order requiring police officers to document abuses by federal immigration agents for potential felony prosecution.</p><p>“We are setting ourselves up, not only for failure. We are setting our officers up for lawsuits that we cannot afford,” Lopez said.</p><p>And the Council agreed to pay <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/11/chicago-police-chase-settlement-27-million-dollars-finance-committee-englewood-crash" target="_blank" >$27 million </a>to the family of a mother of six killed in a high-speed police chase — nearly triple the amount awarded by a jury — amid warnings that new evidence in the case exposed taxpayers to a settlement “well over $100 million.”</p><p>Stacy Vaughn-Harrell, 47, and her then 21-year-old daughter, Kimberlyn Myers, were driving home in June 2017 — after Myers sang at a performance in Indiana — when they were hit by a car that was fleeing police through a residential area in Englewood at a speed of roughly 50 mph. Vaughn-Harrell was killed, and Myers suffered serious injuries, including a concussion, a lacerated liver and a broken collarbone requiring a plate and five screws.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Juliana Stratton derrota a Raja Krishnamoorthi en elección primaria por puesto del senador Dick Durbin
entries.summary: <p>La vicegobernadora Juliana Stratton ha ganado la competitiva primaria demócrata para reemplazar al senador estadounidense Dick Durbin, impulsada por un auge en su campaña y millones en apoyo de su compañero de muchos años, el gobernador de Illinois JB Pritzker.</p><p>El impulso de Stratton fue suficiente para superar los asombrosos $29 millones que el representante Raja Krishnamoorthi gastó en anuncios de campaña. Todo comenzó con una actuación dominante en el debate de enero y se amplificó a través de anuncios sólidos en las últimas semanas de la campaña.</p><p>Stratton superó a Krishnamoorthi en la región de Metro East, el Condado de Sangamon, Chicago y algunos suburbios del Condado de Cook, lo que le quitó cualquier ventaja que el congresista tuviera en algunos condados cercanos. Stratton lideraba a Krishnamoorthi 39.7% a 33.4% con el 85% de los votos contados. La representante estadounidense Robin Kelly se encontraba en tercer lugar con 18.4%.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-4c0000" name="module-4c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"Lo logramos esta noche. Demostramos lo que es posible cuando escuchas al pueblo y le das al pueblo lo que quiere", dijo Stratton en su fiesta de noche electoral en North Lawndale. Llamó a Pritzker, a la senadora Tammy Duckworth y a otros aliados políticos, "socios y amigos inquebrantables que me han animado y han estado a mi lado día tras día".</p><p>"Estamos listos para retomar nuestra democracia en nuestras propias manos", comentó Stratton sobre su victoria.</p><p>La carrera por el Senado sirvió como una prueba del poder político de Pritzker mientras explora su propio futuro, con el gobernador demócrata apoyándola justo un día después de que ella anunciara su candidatura en abril. El gobernador le otorgó $5 millones a un Comité de Acción Política (PAC) a favor de Stratton, lo que fue una fuerte reprimenda a los críticos que cuestionaron si él ayudaría a impulsar su candidatura financieramente.</p><p>También fue una prueba para los demócratas nacionales en el mensaje contra el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE), especialmente en el área de Chicago, donde los residentes fueron afectados en gran medida por el Operativo Midway Blitz de la administración de Trump el año pasado. Stratton fue la única candidata que apoyó la abolición completa de ICE, ofreciendo una postura más progresista para los votantes. Krishnamoorthi prometió apoyo para "abolir el ICE de Trump", la misma posición que Pritzker.</p><p>Illinois verá a su cuarto senador negro en el Senado si en noviembre Stratton gana el escaño que tradicionalmente es demócrata. También se convertiría en la sexta mujer negra en servir en el Senado actual.</p><p>Stratton sirvió en la Asamblea General de Illinois antes de que Pritzker la eligiera como su compañera de fórmula en 2018. Pritzker ganó una carrera primaria demócrata sin oposición el martes para su tercer mandato en el cargo.</p><p>"El coraje me inspiró a postularme. El coraje impulsó esta campaña y el coraje llevará esta lucha directamente a la puerta de Donald Trump", dijo Stratton el martes por la noche. "Lucharemos por Medicare para todos. Lucharemos para abolir ICE. Lucharemos por un salario real que permita vivir, no solo por el mínimo. Lucharemos para defender nuestros derechos y recuperar nuestra democracia del precipicio... Mañana el trabajo comienza de nuevo”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1e0000" name="image-1e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6c92288/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Ff5%2Fb42e446c4111b68370a6ffc79d37%2Fussenate-260318-55.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ffc7be8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Ff5%2Fb42e446c4111b68370a6ffc79d37%2Fussenate-260318-55.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="La vicegobernadora Juliana Stratton, candidata demócrata al Senado por Illinois, pronunció un discurso de victoria en la fiesta de celebración de su elección en los estudios CineCity de Chicago." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ffc7be8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Ff5%2Fb42e446c4111b68370a6ffc79d37%2Fussenate-260318-55.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3443443/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Ff5%2Fb42e446c4111b68370a6ffc79d37%2Fussenate-260318-55.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ffc7be8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Ff5%2Fb42e446c4111b68370a6ffc79d37%2Fussenate-260318-55.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>La vicegobernadora Juliana Stratton, candidata demócrata al Senado por Illinois, pronunció un discurso de victoria en la fiesta de celebración de su elección en los estudios CineCity de Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Stratton apoya un plan de salario mínimo de $25 la hora, mientras que Krishnamoorthi y Kelly apoyaron un plan de $17 la hora, que dijeron tendría una posibilidad más realista de aprobarse en el Senado.</p><p>En su discurso de concesión, Krishnamoorthi agradeció a su familia y a sus apoyos y llamó al país "el mejor país en la Tierra por darle a un niño inmigrante y su familia la oportunidad de vivir el sueño americano".</p><p>"Sólo en este país podría mi historia, y sólo en este país podría un niño como yo servir en los pasillos del Congreso", dijo Krishnamoorthi. "Y ahora debemos unirnos como demócratas y como estadounidenses para asegurarnos de que volvamos a los principios que nos hicieron un faro de libertad y oportunidad para el mundo”.</p><p>A medida que la primaria se ajustaba, los súper PAC comenzaron a inflar la carrera para tratar de ayudar a Krishnamoorthi. La pérdida del congresista también sirve como una prueba de cuán efectivos son los anuncios de campaña a unos nueve meses de la jornada electoral. En total, The Impact Fund —con donantes a favor de Krishnamoorthi, Progressive Values Illinois, PACs pro-cripto Protect Progress y Fairshake, la Asociación de Vicegobernadores Demócratas y el PAC pro-Stratton Illinois Future— gastó más de $16.85 millones en la carrera por el Senado, según un análisis del Sun-Times.</p><p>Fairshake, financiado principalmente por mega donantes y socios empresariales de Trump, Marc Andreessen y Ben Horowitz, gastó más de $5.5 millones, ejecutando anuncios en contra de Stratton. AdImpact, que rastrea anuncios reservados por estaciones de televisión, tiene el gasto total de Fairshake en $8.2 millones.</p><p>Los gritos estallaron en la fiesta electoral de Stratton en North Lawndale cuando las encuestas mostraron que estaba en la delantera a las 9 p.m. Charles Rushing, de 50 años, de Hyde Park, dijo que conoce a Stratton personalmente. Pero comentó que, a pesar de su preferencia personal, ella era la mejor candidata. Stratton cuenta con el respaldo de Pritzker y él está haciendo un "gran trabajo", dijo Rushing.</p><p>Rushing, veterano del ejército estadounidense de 20 años, llevaba una calcomanía que decía "F*CK Trump Vote Juliana". No es "un fan de Donald Trump", especialmente como veterano del ejército estadounidense de 20 años, dijo Rushing. Las acciones militares de Trump son "un delito que merece juicio político", agregó.</p><p>Mientras tanto, Stratton es "honesta, confiable y cumple con sus compromisos, tanto personales como profesionales", añadió Rushing.</p><p>En la fiesta de la noche electoral de Krishnamoorthi en el centro de Chicago, Iqbal Brainch, de Wilmette, trató de mantenerse positivo mientras se hundían sus esperanzas.</p><p>"No se está viendo como habíamos esperado en este momento", dijo Brainch.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-250000" name="image-250000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/088c171/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F40%2F2039d4df4b31a746335f5d1a5bde%2Fussenate-260318-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/527b64a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F40%2F2039d4df4b31a746335f5d1a5bde%2Fussenate-260318-09.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c5396c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F40%2F2039d4df4b31a746335f5d1a5bde%2Fussenate-260318-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="El representante Raja Krishnamoorthi, candidato demócrata a las primarias del Senado de Estados Unidos, abandona el escenario." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c5396c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F40%2F2039d4df4b31a746335f5d1a5bde%2Fussenate-260318-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55cac2a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F40%2F2039d4df4b31a746335f5d1a5bde%2Fussenate-260318-09.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c5396c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F46%2F40%2F2039d4df4b31a746335f5d1a5bde%2Fussenate-260318-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El representante Raja Krishnamoorthi, candidato demócrata a las primarias del Senado de Estados Unidos, abandona el escenario tras reconocer su derrota en la celebración de la noche electoral el martes en el Hotel Westin de River North. Krishnamoorthi perdió su candidatura al Senado frente a la vicegobernadora Juliana Stratton.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Como un estadounidense de origen indio, Brainch dijo que ver a Krishnamoorthi convertirse en el segundo senador estadounidense de origen indio habría sido un paso en la dirección correcta y representaría a un grupo de personas ignoradas.</p><p>"Él ha sido un defensor de nuestra comunidad", dijo. "Piensas en 2016 y esa elección sucedió y Hillary [Clinton] estaba a punto de romper el techo de cristal... de esa manera, él está rompiendo el techo de cristal para los estadounidenses de origen indio en la política”.</p><p>Stratton tuvo dos grandes puntos de inflexión en su campaña, incluido un primer debate en enero en el que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/01/26/senate-debate-illinois-krishnamoorthi-stratton-kelly" target="_blank" >atacó a Krishnamoorthi</a>. Otro impulso llegó con la inyección de efectivo de Pritzker al PAC Illinois Future, que le permitió difundir anuncios para apoyarla y criticar a Krishnamoorthi.</p><p>El Sun-Times informó en diciembre que Krishnamoorthi aceptó más de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/08/raja-krishnamoorthi-senate-campaign-donors-trump-allies-maga-money" target="_blank" >$90,000 en contribuciones de donantes clave</a> del presidente Donald Trump y MAGA, incluido el director de tecnología de Palantir, Shyam Sankar. Palantir tiene un contrato de $30 millones con ICE para proporcionar herramientas para rastrear la autodeportación. La empresa ha sido contratista de ICE desde 2011.</p><p>Krishnamoorthi finalmente donó $33,000 a tres grupos de derechos de inmigrantes, pero Stratton criticó frecuentemente a Krishnamoorthi por las donaciones en debates y anuncios.</p><p>Por su parte, Stratton fue atacada por la campaña de Krishnamoorthi por prometer no aceptar dinero de PAC corporativos, pero <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/21/juliana-stratton-us-senate-bid-march-primary-corporate-pac-money" target="_blank" >proviene de una larga historia de aceptar contribuciones corporativas</a>. También fue criticada por un informe de CBS que destacó que un PAC alineado con Stratton recibió una donación de seis cifras de CoreCivic, contratista de ICE.</p><p>Kelly, quien ha representado al segundo distrito congresional desde 2013, observó la disputa Krishnamoorthi-Stratton y se mantuvo en gran medida al margen. Su último anuncio de televisión se llamó "Distracción" y la mostró mirando anuncios de ataque de los dos y declarando, "Oh, diablos, no".</p><p>Kelly renunció a su puesto para postularse para el Senado, por lo que su pérdida marca el final de su carrera congresional. En un discurso de concesión, Kelly dijo que estaba orgullosa de la campaña que realizó.</p><p>"Yo sé que estoy del lado correcto de los temas. Lo que fue muy importante para mí cuando empecé a postularme fue que dije que tenía que poder mirarme al espejo", dijo Kelly. "Todavía puedo mirarme al espejo".</p><p>Durbin, en un comunicado, dijo que espera "pasar la antorcha" a Stratton al final de su mandato.</p><p>"Ahora nuestra atención debe centrarse en asegurar que Juliana gane la elección general el 3 de noviembre", dijo Durbin. "Con Donald Trump en la Casa Blanca por otros dos años, los desafíos que enfrenta nuestro país y estado seguirán siendo históricos y sin precedentes. Necesitamos a Juliana Stratton luchando junto a la senadora Duckworth todos los días a partir del 3 de enero de 2027”.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/steve-botsford-jr-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Steve Botsford Jr</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/sean-brown-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Sean Brown</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/awisi-a-bustos-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Awisi A. Bustos</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/jonathan-dean-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Jonathan Dean</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/bryan-maxwell-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Bryan Maxwell</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/kevin-ryan-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Kevin Ryan</a> y <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/candidate-questionnaires/christopher-swann-illinois-primary-united-states-senate" target="_blank" >Christopher Swann</a> completaron el grupo de candidatos demócratas.</p><p>En la primaria republicana para el escaño del Senado, el ex presidente del Partido Republicano de Illinois, Don Tracy, fue declarado ganador con el 40%, frente a Jeannie Evans, que obtuvo el 22.6%, con el 65% de los votos contados.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Araceli Gómez-Aldana, WBEZ </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Starting rotation is White Sox' X-factor in 2026: 'Our thing is making sure we give our offense a chance'
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — White Sox manager Will Venable had been coy about his rotation this spring.</p><p>But on Tuesday, Venable was very laconic and not only revealed the rotation, but also the order in which they will pitch.</p><p>After Opening Day starter Shane Smith, it’ll be right-hander Sean Burke, left-hander Anthony Kay and right-handers Davis Martin and Erick Fedde. Smith, Martin and Kay were virtual locks for the rotation, but Burke — last year’s Opening Day starter — was competing for a spot after an inconsistent season. Fedde was a late addition to the competition after signing a one-year, $1.5 million contract right before camp.</p><p>Fedde and Burke were competing with left-hander Sean Newcomb, and right-handers Mike Vasil and Jonathan Cannon. Vasil will be out for the season, Newcomb is pitching out of the bullpen, and Cannon will start the season in Triple-A Charlotte. The team hopes left-handed prospects Hagen Smith and Noah Schultz debut in the majors this season.</p><p>With the offense set, the rotation will be the X-factor for the Sox this season if they want to avoid a fourth consecutive 100-loss season.</p><p>“We can pitch better than we all showed last year,” Burke said. “Our rotation as a whole is taking on that challenge and trying to lead this team. Our thing is making sure we give our offense a chance to win. I feel like every spring training game, I'm hearing a lot of contact and guys hitting. Our job is just to go in the game and keep putting up zeros for offense.”</p><p>Getz accentuated the lineup by adding Austin Hays and Munetaka Murakami, two hitters who project to provide some slug and help runs score in bunches.</p><p>But the rotation — and the Sox’ defense — has some question marks. For one, the Sox’ outfield defense isn’t going to save many runs this season. Despite their woeful offensive production, Luis Robert Jr. and Michael A. Taylor were both standout defenders who saved balls from finding grass, which is a tremendous luxury given that the Sox have many pitchers who pitch to contact.</p><p>Hays has limited range but a strong arm, Andrew Benintendi's defensive effectiveness has waned as injuries have taken their toll; and Luisangel Acuna is still learning the nuances of the position. For a rotation that had the eighth-highest fly ball rate, that outfield uncertainty isn't promising.</p><p>Since they can’t rely on their defense, the Sox have focused on aspects they can control: walk rate (Sox starters had the third-highest walk rate in the majors) and the running game. Controlling the running game was a major reason they hired Zach Bove as their new pitching coach from the Royals — Kansas City was one of the best at limiting stolen bases. For a Sox team that is still accumulating talent, any advantage is necessary.</p><p>“We've been trying to work [controlling the running game] in spring by just varying holds, doing different picks and giving our catchers a good chance to throw,” Burke said.</p><p>But there are more questions than answers in the rotation. How will Smith perform this year after an All-Star rookie season? How will Kay adjust back to the majors after two years away? Can Burke cut down on the mechanical issues that led to his ineffective outings? Can Fedde rediscover his 2024 form?</p><p>The team's slogan this season is "Feel the momentum." But the old baseball adage is that momentum is only as good as the next day's starting pitcher, and the Sox will discover if their rotation can match the offense and answer the skepticism.<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Finally, a deal: WNBA and players reach CBA agreement
entries.summary: <p>WNBA fans can breathe a sigh of relief. At long last, and at the last possible moment, the league and its players reached a verbal agreement on a new collective bargaining agreement.</p><p>More than 100 hours of negotiations over the past week between the players union and the league resulted in a handshake and a champagne toast. Commissioner Cathy Engelbert told reporters she expects the season to start on time.</p><p>Exact details of the agreement have not been announced, but ESPN reported that the salary cap will rise to $7 million, up from $1.5 million in the previous CBA. Minimum salaries are expected to jump from below $70,000 to more than $300,000 and top salaries for megastars will reach $1.4 million. A source familiar with the negotiations confirmed those numbers to the Sun-Times.</p><p>Most importantly, those numbers will grow as the league’s revenue grows.</p><p>“For the first time, player salaries are tied to a truly meaningful share of league revenue,” union president Nneka Ogwumike said in a statement.</p><p>Revenue sharing was the most <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/wnba/2025/12/04/bloody-battle-to-define-revenue-share-in-the-wnba-continues" >contentious point throughout the 17 months of negotiations</a>. In the previous CBA, players shared in league revenue only if certain targets were met; otherwise, the salary cap increased by about 3% each year. That meant that as the league’s popularity surged, player salaries lagged behind.</p><p>Now, reports indicate players will receive close to 20% of league revenue on average over the life of the deal. That still trails the roughly 50% split in major men’s leagues, but it marks a significant step forward and ensures players benefit from the league’s growth.</p><p>After a term sheet is finalized, the deal still must be ratified by the WNBA Board of Governors.</p><p>There is little time to waste before the May 8 season opener. First comes an expansion draft for the two incoming teams, Portland and Toronto. Then a blockbuster free agency period, with more than 100 veterans available, followed by the college draft on April 13. Much of that activity is expected to unfold in early to mid-April.</p><p>Training camp begins April 19, and the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports" target="_blank" >Sky</a> open May 9 against the Portland Fire.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Organizador comunitario Miguel Alvelo Rivera derrota a representante estatal Jaime Andrade Jr. en lado noroeste
entries.summary: <p>El organizador comunitario Miguel Alvelo Rivera superó al titular, el representante estatal Jaime Andrade Jr., en una carrera muy seguida para representar el distrito 40 en el lado noroeste, superando grandes aportes de dinero que llegaron para Andrade en las semanas previas al Día de Elecciones.</p><p>Fue una de las docenas de carreras para el Senado estatal y representantes de Illinois en la boleta primaria del martes, y se encontraba entre varias contiendas muy reñidas en distritos locales donde los titulares se enfrentaron a desafiantes enérgicos.</p><p>Rivera declaró victoria en una <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DWAmkKrjT4N/?img_index=2" target="_blank" >declaración publicada en redes sociales</a>, enfatizando la gran diferencia en recaudación de fondos entre las dos campañas. Atribuyó su éxito a sus voluntarios y partidarios, quienes mostraron que “la solidaridad vence a la avaricia corporativa”. Rivera se comprometió a llevar su mensaje progresista a Springfield.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-df0000" name="module-df0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Cobrar impuestos a los ricos para financiar nuestras escuelas y hospitales, levantar la prohibición al control de la renta y garantizar que cada familia tenga un hogar, detener los centros de datos de AI (Inteligencia Artificial) y proteger nuestro medio ambiente, y mantenernos a salvo del fascismo”, dijo.</p><p>Rivera se presentó como un forastero progresista, mientras que Andrade confiaba en su trayectoria con los votantes del distrito 40, que incluye un tramo del lado noroeste de Bucktown a Albany Park. La recaudación de fondos de Andrade se disparó desde enero con el gran apoyo de demócratas de Illinois en Springfield y del Partido Demócrata de Illinois. Su campaña recaudó casi $1 millón en donaciones de más de $1,000 desde enero.</p><p>Rivera recaudó $232,000 desde enero, principalmente del Comité de Acción Política (PAC) del Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU) junto con el PAC de la Federación de Maestros de Illinois. Rivera contó con el respaldo de varios funcionarios electos del área, incluida la representante Delia Ramírez, cuyo distrito congresional incluye parte del distrito de Andrade.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Cubs newcomer Edward Cabrera rocked in latest spring outing
entries.summary: <p>SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. – Right-hander Edward Cabrera couldn’t keep the string going, allowing eight runs on seven hits and a walk against the Diamondbacks in his second to last start of the spring, following superb outings by righty Cade Horton and left-hander Shota Imanaga the previous two days.</p><p>The 26-year-old newcomer acquired in a trade with the Marlins, Cabrera was pulled after allowing a homer to Mount Carmel grad Alek Thomas and walking Nolan Arenado two batters later. He received a pat on the shoulder from manager Craig Counsell before heading to the dugout and pounding his fist into his glove on the way.</p><p>After three combined innings, Cabrera returned for the fourth but was pulled again after giving up a home run to Corbin Carroll. He then struck out Thomas before serving up a gap double to Geraldo Perdomo and a double off the wall by Arenado.</p><p>Cabrera downplayed the damage.</p><p>“Felt good. Keep working,” said Cabrera, who had allowed one run in his first three outings covering 8 ⅓ innings. “There were some pitches maybe that I left up a little bit, but other than that it was good.</p><p>“I’m not going to get caught up in [over-analyzing], come back tomorrow and go crazy over things. I know what happened out there, I know what I’ve got to go out and do.”</p><p>Lined up fourth in the rotation, Cabrera has one more start left in camp before the regular season.</p><p>It was hard to top Horton and Imanaga, but Cabrera didn’t come close. Horton struck out 10 in five innings of one-run ball against the Guardians, getting a whopping 21 swings and misses Monday. Imanaga didn’t allow a hit until serving up a solo homer in the fifth inning against the Angels Tuesday, topping Horton’s swing-and-miss trickery with 25 and striking out eight.</p><p></p><p><b>Shota succeed with that heat</b></p><p>Imanaga’s four-seam fastball was averaging 92.1 mph, an extra tick above what he lost last season (90.8). That seems to be making a difference as he searches for his All-Star form (15-3, 2.91) of 2024. Imanaga’s ERA in 2025 was almost a full point higher at 3.73.</p><p>“Velocity helps every pitcher,” manager Craid Counsell said. “That was as good an outing as we’ve seen from Shota. If he pitches like that … that’s a great outing. And against real lineups.”</p><p><b>Suzuki update</b></p><p>Right fielder Seiya Suzuki, who has a sprained right knee, was slated to be fitted for a knee brace and take batting practice Wednesday. He played catch, did leg exercises in a pool at the Cubs training facility and did some upper body weight work Tuesday.</p><p>“He had a good day work-wise [Tuesday],” Counsell said.</p><p>Sunday is the first day Suzuki could land on the 10-day injured list and potentially miss only five games if the stint is backdated, so a decision on his Opening Day status could come as soon as the weekend.</p><p><b>DIAMONDBACKS 16, CUBS 8</b></p><p>Playing right field in his new super utility role, Matt Shaw made two errors, dropping Corbin Carroll’s low fly ball hit directly at him to open the first, leading to an unearned run against roughed up starter Edward Cabrera, and letting a single skip off his glove and roll to the warning track for a two-base error in the fourth. A standout third baseman, Shaw has three errors in right field and one at first base this spring. Shaw doubled and is batting .359 with a .960 OPS.</p><p>• On a 101-degree afternoon, center fielder Kevin Alcantrara (3-for-4) and first baseman Jonathan Long hit back-to-back homers against Kade Strowd. Long, in the mix for an Opening Day roster spot, also walked and scored two runs.</p><p>• Top shortstop prospect Jefferson Rojas hit a grand slam against D-backs starter Merrill Kelly in a five-run third.</p><p>• <b>On deck:</b> Off day Thursday.<br></p><table class="cf wS" role="presentation" style="border-collapse: collapse;" id="rte-29e98d10-2325-11f1-b021-355149548d3d"><tbody><tr><td colspan="1" rowspan="1" class="amq" style="margin: 0px; padding: 0px 16px; vertical-align: top; width: 44px; visibility: hidden;"></td><td colspan="1" rowspan="1" class="amr" style="margin: 0px; padding: 0px; width: 754px;"><br></td></tr></tbody></table><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><table class="cf wS" role="presentation" style="border-collapse: collapse;" id="rte-1cfa3dc0-2325-11f1-b021-355149548d3d"><tbody><tr><td colspan="1" rowspan="1" class="amq" style="margin: 0px; padding: 0px 16px; vertical-align: top; width: 44px; visibility: hidden;"></td><td colspan="1" rowspan="1" class="amr" style="margin: 0px; padding: 0px; width: 754px;"><br></td></tr></tbody></table><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p>
entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Man fatally shoots teen attempting to rob him with gun in West Pullman, police say
entries.summary: <p>A man fatally shot a teenage boy who tried to rob him with a gun Wednesday in West Pullman, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The man, 27, was in the 11700 block of South Normal Avenue at 3:36 p.m. when the boy, 16, approached him with a handgun and demanded his belongings, police said in a statement. The man shot the teen with his own handgun.</p><p>The teen was taken to Christ Medical Center in Oak Lawn where he was pronounced dead, police said. The man was taken in for questioning, and two handguns were recovered at the scene, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: White Sox chairman-in-waiting Justin Ishbia eyeing South Loop rail yard for possible stadium
entries.summary: <p>No room for a White Sox stadium at The 78?</p><p>That might not be a problem for the team’s chairman-in-waiting, Justin Ishbia, who is moving to buy land just across the Chicago River from the South Loop site long coveted by Sox ownership to build a new home.</p><p>Ishbia’s private equity firm Shore Capital Partners “is under contract to acquire the Amtrak rail yard and has begun early planning for a mixed-use development” on the site, which is adjacent to the river south of Roosevelt Road, a spokesperson for the Sox minority owner said Wednesday.</p><p>The spokesperson said Shore Capital is exploring the development of “a potential healthcare facility and medical innovation hub” in partnership with Northwestern Medicine.</p><p>But sources close to the proposal say Ishbia is also mulling a stadium for the 47-acre plot, which neighbors other rail yards that would likely have to change hands in order for any potential Sox project to break ground.</p><p>Ishbia’s spokesperson didn’t confirm the 48-year-old billionaire’s westward stadium exploration, and noted any decisions about the team’s home rest solely with Sox chairman Jerry Reinsdorf. The plan was first reported by Crain’s Chicago Business.</p><p>The team announced <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/06/05/white-sox-jerry-reinsdorf-justin-ishbia-ownership-transfer-new-stadium" target="_blank" ><u>a potential succession plan last year</u></a> that would give Ishbia the option to buy a controlling interest from Reinsdorf as early as 2029, the same year the Sox’ lease at Rate Field is up.</p><p>Sox spokesman Scott Reifert said the team’s focus “has been and continues to be solely on the potential of a new ballpark at The 78.”</p><p>But the Sox have been stymied for two years <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2024/1/17/24042048/white-sox-new-stadium-78-site-south-loop-related-midwest-reinsdorf" target="_blank" ><u>since first floating glitzy renderings</u></a> of a skyline-facing stadium on the east side of the river. Reinsdorf traveled to Springfield himself to lobby state lawmakers for public funding to support his 78 vision to no avail, while the Bears continue to haggle for legislative help to stay in Illinois.</p><p>The Chicago Fire <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/03/chicago-fire-football-club-breaks-ground-650-million-stadium-78" target="_blank" ><u>broke ground earlier this month</u></a> on a $750 million stadium near Roosevelt and Clark — fully funded by billionaire owner Joe Mansueto — and local Ald. Pat Dowell (3rd) has said she’s against trying to squeeze two stadiums into one of the city’s fastest growing neighborhoods.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson said the team has told his office "they want to stay in Chicago. They're looking at different spaces that could make sense for the White Sox, so we're going to continue to have conversations with people. It's clear that all of our sports teams want to stay in Chicago."</p><p>Ishbia has all but guaranteed a new home for the Sox at some point if he takes the reins, and the rail yard site would give the team just as dramatic an outfield view as one from The 78, not to mention the potential for home runs splashing into the river.</p><p>“Whether a new stadium occurs in four years or 34 years, I don’t know, but I’m going to be involved in a stadium at some point with the White Sox,” Ishbia told the Sun-Times last fall <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2025/11/19/justin-ishbia-pope-white-sox-stadium" target="_blank" ><u>after meeting the team’s most famous fan, Pope Leo XIV</u></a>, who has an open invitation to throw out the first pitch.</p><p>Not that any of that could happen anytime soon.</p><p>“I have zero to do with the organization today. I’m a passive owner. And by the way, I don’t want to use the word 'owner.' I use the word 'steward.' For me, Jerry today is the chairman, and he is the steward of the franchise. And one day, I’ll have a good fortune of being the steward,” Ishbia said in November. “My view is, this is a community asset. This belongs to the city of Chicago. It’s my job as steward, when it’s my turn, to do my very best to create wonderful, happy memories and moments for Chicago fans all over the city and all over the world.”</p><p>An Amtrak spokesman said the agency “is pursuing all available opportunities to deliver a larger and improved Midwest maintenance facility that would bring rail assets into a state of good repair, enable faster train turnarounds, and enhance on‑time performance.”</p><p>Rick Harnish, executive director of the High Speed Rail Alliance — a nonprofit advocate for better rail networks — slammed the idea, calling it “unacceptable that Amtrak has been working for months to sell a critical national asset with no public engagement. This sale must be stopped. This property may be needed for access to and through Chicago Union Station to bring hourly passenger rail service to destinations around the Midwest.”</p><p>Northwestern Medicine spokesman Chris King said they’re “in the early stages of discussions about a potential health care facility as part of this development. Consistent with our recent expansions in neighborhoods such as Bronzeville and Old Irving Park, we continue to explore opportunities to bring world‑class, compassionate care closer to where our patients live and work. This potential project would allow us to further expand our footprint in the South Loop.”</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: How far will Illinois go in the NCAA Tournament? Here's how you voted
entries.summary: <p>In an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >NCAA Tournament</a> edition of “Polling Place,” your home for Sun-Times sports polls on the social platform X, we asked how deep No. 3 seed Illinois will go.</p><p>“Completely depends on their commitment to urgency,” @jobin_theman commented. “They have the talent and skill to hang with other elite teams and crush lower teams, but they get complacent. Just need to let the dawgs out.”</p><p>Next, we asked which local program will get back to the Big Dance first: DePaul, Loyola, Northwestern or UIC?</p><p>Last, which postseason is better, college basketball’s or college football’s?</p><p>“The basketball tournament rocks,” @RonaldVoigt4 wrote, “and Vegas during the first two days is electric.”</p><p>On to the polls:</p><p><b>Poll No. 1: How far will Illinois’ men’s basketball team go in the NCAA Tournament?</b></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">It’s time for a March Madness edition of “Polling Place.” Let us hear from you! Selected comments will appear in Thursday's paper.<br><br>Poll No. 1: How far will Illinois’ men’s basketball team go in the NCAA Tournament?</p>— Sun-Times Sports (@suntimes_sports) <a href="https://twitter.com/suntimes_sports/status/2033675131617267740?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p><b>Upshot: </b>If the Illini get to Houston for the Sweet 16, they’ll run up against second-seeded Houston. That’s some tough trip.</p><p><b>Poll No. 2: Which men’s program will get back to the Big Dance first?</b></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Poll No. 2: Which men’s program will get back to the Big Dance first?</p>— Sun-Times Sports (@suntimes_sports) <a href="https://twitter.com/suntimes_sports/status/2033675135429853225?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p><b>Upshot: </b>Do we like what DePaul’s Chris Holtmann is cooking or is this vote more about what the others don’t have on the ol’ stove?</p><p><b>Poll No. 3: Which postseason is better?</b></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Poll No. 3: Which postseason is better?</p>— Sun-Times Sports (@suntimes_sports) <a href="https://twitter.com/suntimes_sports/status/2033675139561226277?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p><b>Upshot: </b>The Big Dance rules, people, but you knew that already.</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Illinois vs. Penn is a Brad Underwood-Fran McCaffery reunion, and nobody's upset — yet
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Illinois</a> is in trouble against Penn in the first round of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/illinois-sweet-16-path-ncaa-tournament-illini-houston-cougars-march-madness" target="_blank" >men’s NCAA Tournament</a>.</p><p>Minor trouble, at the least. Unless it’s no trouble at all.</p><p>Who can be sure?</p><p>Since the tournament expanded to 64 teams in 1985, No. 14 seeds have upset No. 3 seeds 23 times in 160 tries, or 14.4% of the time. So it definitely happens, and it could happen Thursday (8:25 p.m., TNT) in Greenville, South Carolina, even though the Quakers finished third in the Ivy League during the regular season and are on the wrong end of a 24½-point spread.</p><p>Illini coach Brad Underwood isn’t taking anything for granted. That’s because 10 years ago in New York, he witnessed mentor Bob Huggins go down hard as a 3 seed, his West Virginia team smoked 70-56 by 14 seed Stephen F. Austin. Nicknamed “Press Virginia” for the havoc they created defensively, the Mountaineers instead were harassed into 22 turnovers themselves, 15 more than they created. Huggins was just plain outcoached that day — by SFA’s Underwood, who’d worked for Huggins for six seasons at Kansas State.</p><p>It led to this all-timer of a quote from a disgusted Huggins:</p><p>“I don’t know why anybody would waste energy pressing us. We’ll throw it to you regardless.”</p><p>Don’t you just have to love this tournament?</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/illinois-sweet-16-path-ncaa-tournament-illini-houston-cougars-march-madness" >Illinois’ path to Sweet 16 is nice, but then? Trouble — real trouble</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/18/ncaa-tournament-bracket-tips-and-why-illinois-might-be-a-team-that-can-win-the-whole-thing" >NCAA Tournament bracket tips — and why Illinois might be a team that can win the whole thing</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/15/my-ncaa-tournament-pick-arizona-wildcats-big-12-final-four-march-madness" target="_blank" >My NCAA Tournament pick: Arizona will win it all in a Big 12-heavy Final Four</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Another nice story: Former longtime Iowa coach Fran McCaffery, who was fired last year after his 15th season, landed at Penn, his alma mater. At 66, McCaffery — a temperamental coach Illinois fans loved to not love, but also close friends with Underwood — is back home in more ways than one. Penn is in Philadelphia, McCaffery’s hometown.</p><p>The Quakers (18-11) upset Yale to win the Ivy tournament, with 6-9 forward TJ Power — whom McCaffery calls “one of the best players in the country, bar none” — exploding for 44 points and 14 rebounds. They won the tournament without leading scorer Ethan Roberts, who is from Arlington Heights (Hersey). Roberts is in concussion protocol and will not play against the Illini (24-8).</p><p>“If it was his ankle, he’d be playing. Tape it up,” McCaffery said. “But the concussion stuff, you don’t mess with that. His future is too important, and we love him.”</p><p>Even McCaffery’s critics have to admit his track record of getting teams to the Big Dance is impressive. Long ago, he got Lehigh there and it helped lead him to UNC Greensboro. He took UNC Greensboro there and it helped lead him to Siena. He took Siena there three times — winning first-round games twice — and that got him to Iowa. The Hawkeyes went to the tournament seven times under McCaffery.</p><p>Now Penn is dancing for the first time since 2018.</p><p>“What he’s done with that team is just short of amazing,” Underwood said. “They’re good. It’s a good basketball team. I see Fran’s thumbprint all over it, and the competitiveness.”</p><p>By the way, that sound you hear in the distance is, in fact, McCaffery’s critics. They’re pointing out the man has taken teams to the NCAA Tournament 12 times without ever making it past the second round. It’s the truth. Barring a massive upset against Underwood — who was 9-6 head-to-head against McCaffery in Illinois-Iowa games — it’s about to be 13.</p><p>Underwood would seem to hold all the cards in this one.</p><p>“He gets pieces that fit,” McCaffery said. “He gets pieces that prioritize winning, and his teams continue to win. Because, as we know, some teams that build rosters now look good on paper, but they don’t win. His teams win, and there’s a reason for that.”</p><p>But at Bon Secours Wellness Arena — Greenvillians just call it “The Well” — anything might happen. That’s why it’s called March Madness.</p><p>Well, well, well — a 14 over a 3? Or no trouble for the Illini at all?</p><p>It’s that time.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/eCJjC86AZas?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Stephen F. Austin vs. West Virginia: Lumberjacks cruise"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Funeral arrangements announced for Chicago firefighter who died after Rogers Park blaze
entries.summary: <p>A funeral has been scheduled for next week for third generation Chicago firefighter Michael Altman, who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/17/firefighter-seriously-hurt-battling-rogers-park-fire-has-died" target="_blank" >died after being injured fighting a Rogers Park blaze on Monday</a>.</p><p>Visitation will be held 3 p.m. to 8 p.m. March 26 at St. Rita of Cascia Shrine Chapel, 7740 S. Western Ave., the Chicago Fire Department announced Wednesday. Funeral services will be held there at 10 a.m. March 27.</p><p>Altman, who served on Truck 47 in Edgewater, responded to a fire at a four-flat in Rogers Park late Monday morning. When the floor collapsed, he fell into the flames, fire officials said.</p><p>Altman suffered extensive injuries and died Tuesday morning at age 32, authorities said. He had been on the job for less than two years.</p><p>Autopsy results released Wednesday by the Cook County medical examiner's office were pending further analysis.</p><p>Altman’s grandfather, Edward P. Altman, served as the fire commissioner from 1996 until 1999. Three of his sons — Edward F., James and Altman’s father, Michael — followed in his footsteps and were also firefighters. Altman’s father rose to the rank of battalion chief.</p><p>Fire Commissioner Annette Nance-Holt said it’s a “profoundly difficult time for all of us" during a press conference Tuesday outside Stroger Hospital.</p><p>“We are heartbroken by yet another loss of our own,” she said. “Michael gave everything in service to the people of Chicago, and we will never forget his courage and sacrifice.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture
