entries.title: 21 things to do in Chicago March 19-25
entries.summary: <h3>Theater</h3><ul id="rte-185f8420-20ad-11f1-af56-9b0a4db2a081"><li>In Itamar Moses’ darkly funny play, <b>“The Ally,”</b> a well-meaning college professor finds himself engulfed in a firestorm when he signs a student petition condemning police brutality. Jeremy Wechsler directs. From March 20-May 2 at Theater Wit, 1229 W. Belmont Ave. Tickets: $18-$44. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://theaterwit.org" target="_blank" ><u>theaterwit.org</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f80000" name="image-f80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/857d2b7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F61%2Fe8%2F59906ca943668aa68c43ed2f5b12%2Fel-ultimo-sueno-de-frida-y-diego-photo-by-cory-weaver-san-francisco-opera.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ac4b50b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F61%2Fe8%2F59906ca943668aa68c43ed2f5b12%2Fel-ultimo-sueno-de-frida-y-diego-photo-by-cory-weaver-san-francisco-opera.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3ec5c4d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F61%2Fe8%2F59906ca943668aa68c43ed2f5b12%2Fel-ultimo-sueno-de-frida-y-diego-photo-by-cory-weaver-san-francisco-opera.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="El último sueño de Frida y Diego - photo by Cory Weaver_San Francisco Opera.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3ec5c4d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F61%2Fe8%2F59906ca943668aa68c43ed2f5b12%2Fel-ultimo-sueno-de-frida-y-diego-photo-by-cory-weaver-san-francisco-opera.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/01290a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F61%2Fe8%2F59906ca943668aa68c43ed2f5b12%2Fel-ultimo-sueno-de-frida-y-diego-photo-by-cory-weaver-san-francisco-opera.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3ec5c4d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F61%2Fe8%2F59906ca943668aa68c43ed2f5b12%2Fel-ultimo-sueno-de-frida-y-diego-photo-by-cory-weaver-san-francisco-opera.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“El último sueño de Frida y Diego” runs from March 21-April 4 at Lyric Opera, 20 N. Wacker Drive.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Cory Weaver/San Francisco Opera</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul id="rte-185f8421-20ad-11f1-af56-9b0a4db2a081"><li>Gabriela Lena Frank and Nilo Cruz’s <b>“El último sueño de Frida y Diego” </b>reunites artists Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera in a dreamlike journey through memories of love, passion and art with music inspired by Mexican folk traditions. From March 21-April 4 at Lyric Opera, 20 N. Wacker Drive. Tickets: $52+. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://lyricopera.org" target="_blank" ><u>lyricopera.org</u></a>.</li><li>The Gift Theatre begins its 25th anniversary season with <b>“Ten 25th,”</b> a festival of 10-minute plays by Chicago playwrights John Gawlik, Jennifer Rumberger, Gregory Fenner, Kimberly Dixon-Mays, Dolores Diaz, Stephanie Alison Walker, Emilio Williams, Jermaine Jenkins and Brett Neveu. From March 25-April 4 at A Red Orchid Theatre, 1531 N. Wells St. Tickets: $25. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://thegifttheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>thegifttheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Preston Max Allen’s drama <b>“Modern Gentleman”</b> is the story of 28-year-old Adam who after a breakup must navigate the world of dating for the first time since coming out as a trans man and finally confront his complicated relationship to his identity. Landree Fleming directs. From March 19-April 18 at Raven Theatre, 6157 N. Clark St. Tickets: $25-$75. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://aboutfacetheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>aboutfacetheatre.com</u></a>.</li><li>Stanislaw I. Witkiewicz’s <b>“The Cuttlefish, or the Hyrcanian Worldview”</b> is a satire about an artist torn between integrity and survival, freedom and obedience. Nicole Wiesner directs. From March 19-April 25 at Trap Door Theatre, 1655 W. Cortland St. Tickets: $32. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://trapdoortheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>trapdoortheatre.com</u></a>.</li><li>In <b>“The Posthumous Trial of Giulia Tofana</b>,” playwright Gwyneth Forsythe digs into the story of the creator of the Aqua Tofana poison who in 1659 was executed for the murder of 600 men. Sydney Genco stars; Cheryl Snodgrass directs. From March 20-April 5 at The Den Theatre, 1331 N. Milwaukee Ave. Tickets: $25. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://thedentheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>thedentheatre.com</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b10000" name="image-b10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba05fe1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1883x1057+0+884/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F5a%2Ff4dae5cf4015bc29af2b9eb9f5f7%2Fluis-carreon-photo-by-sarah-elizabeth-larson.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/496cbc7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1883x1057+0+884/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F5a%2Ff4dae5cf4015bc29af2b9eb9f5f7%2Fluis-carreon-photo-by-sarah-elizabeth-larson.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/06d49aa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1883x1057+0+884/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F5a%2Ff4dae5cf4015bc29af2b9eb9f5f7%2Fluis-carreon-photo-by-sarah-elizabeth-larson.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Luis Carreon - 19-Dec. 15 at Jane Addams Hull House Museum, 800 S. Halsted St.</p></figcaption></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul id="rte-185f8427-20ad-11f1-af56-9b0a4db2a081"><li><b>“Perennial City: Experiments in Urban Gardening”</b> uncovers the deep roots of Chicago’s urban gardening movement in the histories of immigrant communities and the Hull-House reformers who advocated for reform during the early 20th century. Includes archival materials, new art commissions and an installation produced by Red Line Service artists in a workshop facilitated by Melissa Hilliard Potter. From March 19-Dec. 15 at Jane Addams Hull House Museum, 800 S. Halsted St. Admission is free. entries.author: Mary Houlihan

entries.title: Gov. JB Pritzker flexes political muscle through Juliana Stratton's decisive Senate primary win
entries.summary: <p>Gov. JB Pritzker helped push Lt. Gov. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/us-senate-illinois-primary-results" target="_blank" ><u>Juliana Stratton</u></a> across the finish line for her Senate primary win, and his endorsement boosted <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/illinois-comptroller-democratic-primary-results" target="_blank" ><u>Margaret Croke</u></a> for state comptroller too.</p><p>But the billionaire Democratic governor on Wednesday brushed aside national media coverage casting him as Illinois’ kingmaker, as Pritzker weighs a presidential run and gears up for another battle with Republican gubernatorial nominee <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/governor-illinois-republican-race-primary-results?55" target="_blank" ><u>Darren Bailey</u></a>.</p><p>“Juliana won this because she traveled the state. She went and won people over, and I think her authenticity, her genuineness is what broke through,” Pritzker told the Chicago Sun-Times after flexing his political muscle on election night.</p><p>“I believe that the voters understood that all of that negative advertising that got run against her was not real, that they believed who she is, that she stood up and had bold ideas. She told people she's going to go fight for them, and they understood that she's been fighting for them here in Illinois,” the governor said of his two-time running mate who campaigned on their record together.</p><p>“She did this on her own. I wasn't out on the campaign trail with her. I went to maybe a few places where I was asked to go. But I'm really proud. I watched her as a candidate — people reacted so well to her. And I think she, frankly, outshined the other candidates, and that's why she won,” Pritzker said.</p><p>Still, he helped amplify Stratton’s message in the home stretch of primary season, shaking hands alongside her and appearing in ads that were bankrolled by a super PAC he funded to the tune of $5 million. That helped her maintain footing despite being vastly out-fundraised and outspent by U.S. Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi’s campaign, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/18/illinois-primary-super-pac-spending-aipac-cryptocurrency-ai-sports-betting" target="_blank" ><u>opponents in the cryptocurrency industry</u></a> spending some $10 million against her. </p><p>With unprecedented super PAC dollars flowing into Chicago area congressional races this year, Pritzker said he’s felt a responsibility to tap his own fortune to counter major spending by outside forces.</p><p>“We have a system that isn't working, but it's one where you can't unilaterally disarm. And meanwhile, here we had all these special interests who had for-profit motives, some of them had a motive about some single issue that they're focused on, and it brought tens of millions of dollars into the state of Illinois that had nothing to do directly with the candidates,” Pritzker said. “From my particular point of view, trying to counteract that is what we ought to be all about, and it's what I've tried to do throughout my career.”</p><p>With a clear path charted to Washington for Stratton through the general election against Republican nominee Don Tracy in deep-blue Illinois, Pritzker can take at least some credit for elevating the state’s second-ever Black woman senator, whether he or not he publicly acknowledges that — or his possible White House ambitions.</p><p>Instead he says he’s laser-focused on his rematch with Bailey, who wiped out the GOP governor field just like he did four years ago before losing mightily to Pritzker, who didn’t have a primary opponent in his bid for a third term.</p><p>In his primary victory speech, Bailey slammed Pritzker as an elite “who decrees from the top down and looks down on the people who actually make this state work.”</p><p>The downstate farmer also positioned himself as a changed candidate from 2022, reflecting that the deaths last fall of his son, daughter-in-law and two grandchildren in a helicopter crash “changed me forever.”</p><p>Pritzker has shared condolences to Bailey’s family following the immeasurable tragedy. But he said voters won’t find a new man atop the Republican ticket.</p><p>“[Bailey] has said himself he needed to change his message. He's been saying that all along. ... He's never said he's going to change his views, and he hasn't. Is it the way he's going to deliver on the question of whether abortion is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2022/8/10/23300802/pritzker-bailey-apology-holocaust-survivors-abortion-comparison-jewish-community-roe-v-wade-fair" target="_blank" ><u>like the Holocaust</u></a>? Is he now changed? Is he now pro-choice?” Pritzker asked of the GOP nominee, a staunchly anti-abortion rights candidate.</p><p>“Does he think it's OK, in the wake of a tragedy where people are gunned down in Highland Park, to say it's time to move on, and not to actually do something about it?” the governor asked, pointing to another comment Bailey <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2022/7/5/23195364/darren-bailey-highland-park-move-on-republican-governor-candidate-downstate-pritzker" target="_blank" ><u>had to walk back during his ‘22 campaign</u></a>.</p><p>“I don't think he's changed the views that he's had all along,” Pritzker said. “I think he wants to couch it in different language and messaging, and then hope that people will buy into his messaging, but not pay any attention to what his actual positions are on these issues.”</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Fed projects one rate cut this year as Powell vows to stay on until DOJ investigation is finished
entries.summary: <p>Federal Reserve officials expect the Iran war will worsen inflation this year while having little impact on economic growth, but they still expect to cut their key rate once in 2026.</p><p>For now, Fed policymakers left short-term interest rates unchanged Wednesday for the second straight meeting at about 3.6%. The central bank said in a statement that the “implications of developments in the Middle East for the U.S. economy are uncertain.”</p><p>Still, by keeping their forecast for a rate cut this year and next — the same projections that they made in December — central bank policymakers appear to expect the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/03/04/chicagoans-uneasy-gas-prices-iran-war-straight-hormuz-supply-chain-disruptions" >gas price spike</a> from the Iran war to have a largely temporary effect on inflation and the economy. Policymakers also foresee unemployment remaining unchanged by the end of this year, a more optimistic outlook than most outside economists.</p><p>Whether that turns out to be true will largely depend on the length of the conflict in the Middle East. The officials expect inflation to fall back to 2.2% in 2027 and hit the Fed’s 2% target in 2028.</p><p>Speaking to reporters after the rate decision was announced, Fed Chair Jerome Powell maintained a largely optimistic outlook, pointing out that in recent years the economy has been hit with numerous shocks — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/02/20/supreme-court-trump-sweeping-tariffs-john-roberts-economic-agenda" >tariffs</a>, the Fed's own rate hikes in 2022 and 2023, the aftermath of the pandemic — and has avoided recession all along.</p><p>“The U.S. economy has been doing really well through a lot of challenges,” Powell said. “It’s been amazing to see.”</p><p>Powell did clarify a key question about the Fed's future: He said he has “no intention” of leaving the central bank until an investigation into his congressional testimony about the Fed's building renovation is dropped. Last Friday, a judge threw out a pair of subpoenas that the Justice Department had issued to the Fed, dealing a blow to the investigation. But U.S. Attorney Jeannine Pirro has said she will appeal the ruling.</p><p>Powell’s term as Fed chair is scheduled to end on May 15, and President Donald Trump has nominated a former top Fed official, Kevin Warsh, as his replacement. Warsh’s nomination has been delayed in the Senate because key Republican senators are opposed to the DOJ probe.</p><p>After the investigation is resolved and even after Warsh is confirmed, Powell could elect to stay on the board to finish his term as a Fed governor, which lasts until January 2028. But he told reporters he had not yet made that decision.</p><p>With the economy's future so uncertain, Powell underscored that any further cuts to rates this year were hardly locked in.</p><p>“The rate forecast is conditional on the performance of the economy, so if we don’t see that progress then you won't see the rate cut,” he said.</p><p>In the Fed's quarterly economic projections, also released Wednesday, officials only modestly raised their forecasts for inflation, and now expect it will end this year at 2.7%, up from their December forecast but slightly below the 2.8% it reached in January. They expect core inflation, which excludes the volatile food and energy categories, to also finish the year at 2.7%.</p><p>Fed officials slightly boosted their outlook for growth this year and expected unemployment to stay unchanged at 4.4%.</p><p>Tim Duy, chief economist at SGH Macro, said the forecasts were essentially “stale” as policymakers avoided fully taking into account the impacts of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/02/28/iran-trump-israel" >Iran war</a> on the economy.</p><p>The Fed considers core prices a better measure of longer-run inflation. Consumer prices will spike higher in the coming months as gas prices have soared, but those increases could unwind by the end of the year, particularly if the conflict ends soon.</p><p></p><p>On Wall Street, losses for stocks deepened after the Fed's decision. The S&P 500 fell 1.4% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 768 points, or 1.6%.</p><p>Gas prices jumped Wednesday to a nationwide average of $3.84 a gallon, according to AAA, up 92 cents from a month ago. The increase will push inflation much higher in March, but core inflation, since it excludes gas, could be much less affected.</p><p>Typically, the Fed would look past a supply shock like the disruption in oil supplies from the Middle East and its impact on inflation. Once it ends, any inflation it produces may fall back, without the Fed having to raise rates. As a result, the Fed could leave rates unchanged — or even cut them to boost weak hiring.</p><p></p><p></p><p>Even before the Iran war, problems had cropped up in both the inflation and jobs data, putting the Fed in a tight spot. Prices rose more quickly in January than in recent months, according to the Fed's preferred measure, with inflation excluding food and energy reaching 3.1% compared with a year earlier. That is little changed from where it was two years ago, a sign that prices are still rising at a stubbornly elevated pace.</p><p>Businesses and other employers shed 92,000 jobs in February, the government reported this month, an unexpectedly weak showing that followed an encouraging gain of 130,000 in January. The unemployment rate ticked higher to a still-low 4.4% from 4.3%.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/03/04/chicagoans-uneasy-gas-prices-iran-war-straight-hormuz-supply-chain-disruptions" >Chicagoans uneasy about gas prices as Iran war disrupts supply chain</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Christopher Rugaber | AP

entries.title: Frankfort man arrested, accused of threatening Gov. Pritzker
entries.summary: <p>A suburban man has been arrested for allegedly threatening Gov. JB Pritzker, according to Illinois State Police.</p><p>Timothy J. Shemitis, a 71-year-old Frankfort resident, faces a felony count of threatening a public official and one misdemeanor charge of harassment by telephone, police said.</p><p>Police were alerted of the voicemails left with Pritzker’s office March 3, and a warrant was issued a week later, police said. Shemitis was arrested Wednesday, according to court records.</p><p>Shemitis is due in court again Friday.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: City Council freezes pay for tipped workers at 76% of minimum wage to help struggling restaurants
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s tipped workers could have their hourly pay frozen at 76% of the city’s minimum wage, thanks to a City Council vote Wednesday that throws an economic bone to restaurants, but sets the stage for Mayor Brandon Johnson’s third veto.</p><p>At a time when Chicago is becoming increasingly unaffordable for working-class residents,<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/16/mayor-brandon-johnson-subminimum-wage-phaseout-tipped-workers" > Johnson said</a> this week it would be “not only tone deaf, but irresponsible” for the City Council to stop what was supposed to be a five-year phase-out of the subminimum wage for tipped workers.</p><p>The mayor counts the phase-out  as one of the signature wins of his progressive agenda. He plans to fight to keep it — even if it opens a new front in his ongoing battle with an emboldened City Council majority that rejected his proposed corporate head tax.</p><p>Thirty-four votes are needed to override a mayoral veto. The vote to freeze the phase-out was 30 to 18.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-420000" name="image-420000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/baf8e1a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5079x2850+0+268/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fe0%2F38984d524a98bd5c63fb1c38d9af%2Fcouncil-260319-15.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc2ce31/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5079x2850+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fe0%2F38984d524a98bd5c63fb1c38d9af%2Fcouncil-260319-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Mayor Brandon Johnson presides over a Chicago City Council meeting at City Hall on Wednesday." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc2ce31/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5079x2850+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fe0%2F38984d524a98bd5c63fb1c38d9af%2Fcouncil-260319-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e92fe37/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5079x2850+0+268/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fe0%2F38984d524a98bd5c63fb1c38d9af%2Fcouncil-260319-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc2ce31/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5079x2850+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2Fe0%2F38984d524a98bd5c63fb1c38d9af%2Fcouncil-260319-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson presides over a Chicago City Council meeting at City Hall on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Johnson called Wednesday's vote "shameful" and said he was not about to "stand idly by and watch Black and Brown women suffer at the hands of corporate interests." He added, "If I've got to veto something to make sure that Black and Brown women are protected, then veto it is."</p><p>The mayor said Lt. Gov. Julianna Stratton won the Democratic primary for the U.S. Senate on a promise to work to raise the minimum wage to $25-an-hour. "There is like a real disconnect going on in City Council now. It's like an alt-universe... The entire world is calling for affordability and raising wages, and this City Council just took raises away from Black and Brown women."</p><p></p><p></p><p>Johnson has already vetoed an ordinance that would have empowered police to issue snap curfews and another that banned the sale of most hemp products in Chicago. This time, he’s almost certain to have a bigger fight on his hands.</p><p>The Illinois Restaurant Association has spent the last year building support to stop the five-year phase-out and freeze the so-called “tip credit” at 24% of Chicago’s minimum wage, which now stands at $16.60 an hour.</p><p>Without the freeze, tipped workers now paid $12.62 an hour — or 24% less than the $16.60 paid to hourly Chicago workers who do not receive tips — would receive a raise to 16% of Chicago’s minimum wage. That amount is re-set every July 1. With the freeze, they would still receive a raise, but it would be capped at 76% of whatever the minimum hourly wage turns out to be.</p><p></p><p>Restaurants fighting for survival have joined the campaign armed with surveys that underscore what they claim is an urgent need for economic relief for an industry that has not fully recovered from the pandemic. Those surveys show that nearly 500 Chicago restaurants closed during the first half of 2025, and that the total workforce at the city’s full-service restaurants remains 7,800 jobs below pre-pandemic levels.</p><p>Customers also are paying higher prices for reduced service. Nearly 90% of restaurants surveyed say they’ve raised menu prices, while 72% have cut staff and 79% have reduced employee hours.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ee0000" name="image-ee0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/26ae3ca/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F52%2F7c98dabd44bd822310f15c2f1dc5%2Fcouncil-260319-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3f8ab66/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F52%2F7c98dabd44bd822310f15c2f1dc5%2Fcouncil-260319-09.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d14627/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F52%2F7c98dabd44bd822310f15c2f1dc5%2Fcouncil-260319-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ald. Anthony Beale (9th) speaks Wednesday during a Chicago City Council meeting." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d14627/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F52%2F7c98dabd44bd822310f15c2f1dc5%2Fcouncil-260319-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2dba19a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F52%2F7c98dabd44bd822310f15c2f1dc5%2Fcouncil-260319-09.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d14627/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F52%2F7c98dabd44bd822310f15c2f1dc5%2Fcouncil-260319-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ald. Anthony Beale (9th) speaks Wednesday during a Chicago City Council meeting.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Far South Side Ald. Anthony Beale (9th) said Wednesday he’s convinced that, without a break, more restaurants that are the lifeblood of Chicago neighborhoods will be forced to either close altogether or move to tax-friendly suburbs. That would mean more gaping holes in neighborhood commercial strips that already have many vacant storefronts, Beale said.</p><p>"Businesses are closing the doors. It's okay to admit we were wrong. We need to correct the wrong to help save businesses on life support," he said.</p><p></p><p></p><p>Mayoral allies claimed that the affordability crisis now dominating politics in Chicago and around the nation makes it precisely the wrong time to deny a workforce in an industry dominated by women of color the right to earn at least as much as their counterparts in other industries.</p><p>"This is a huge mistake, you all," said Ald. Anthony Quezada (35th), whose parents were restaurant employees. "There is a real honest rage coming from working class people. We are putting the most vulnerable workers at risk. That is a shame, you all."</p><p>Progressive firebrand Byron Sigcho-Lopez (25th) was virtually screaming at his colleagues.</p><p>"It's unbelievable that we're even discussing this. Have you seen what inflation looks like for tipped workers?" he said. "One of the best things that this City Council has done is actually support these tipped workers so they have a dignified wage that, even then, cannot keep up with inflation... We've got to do more for small businesses. But not at the expense of tipped workers."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0d0000" name="image-0d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cfc1170/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F19%2Feab783ec4cc39fc64c7b7326d5ab%2Fcouncil-260319-25.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba67a91/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F19%2Feab783ec4cc39fc64c7b7326d5ab%2Fcouncil-260319-25.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/808c727/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F19%2Feab783ec4cc39fc64c7b7326d5ab%2Fcouncil-260319-25.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ald. Byron Sigcho-Lopez (25th) speaks Wednesday during a Chicago City Council meeting at City Hall." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/808c727/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F19%2Feab783ec4cc39fc64c7b7326d5ab%2Fcouncil-260319-25.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55a266e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F19%2Feab783ec4cc39fc64c7b7326d5ab%2Fcouncil-260319-25.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/808c727/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fac%2F19%2Feab783ec4cc39fc64c7b7326d5ab%2Fcouncil-260319-25.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ald. entries.title: Cubs reliever Daniel Palencia is on top of the World
entries.summary: <p>SCOTTSDALE, Ariz. – Daniel Palencia’s Cubs teammates watched intensely as he retired Team USA in order in the ninth inning of the World Baseball Classic Tuesday night.</p><p>Cubs management, knowing the experience for their closer was perhaps a once-in-a-lifetime moment that should bode well for the 26-year-old’s confidence, also watched with mild trepidation knowing the triple-digit flamethrower they call “La Gasolina” was emptying his tank with full-throttle effort before the season has begun.</p><p>They also had to be thinking, “Don’t hurt him!” as teammates mobbed Palencia, who had hurled his glove toward the sky with two hands and dropped to his knees after striking out Roman Anthony on a 99.7 mph pitch to finish a 3-2 victory for underdog Venezuela. When all was said and done, there was no getting around the joy felt for this Cub.</p><p>“I was really happy, we all were happy for Danny,” manager Craig Counsell said Wednesday. “That’s why you play in the tournament, for moments like that.”</p><p>Cubs left-hander Shota Imanaga yelled out watching the last out live on a Cubs media relations person’s phone, seconds after finishing interviews following his outstanding start against the Angels at Sloan Park Tuesday night. Cubs catcher Moises Ballesteros, who had slammed a pair of 400-plus foot home runs an hour or so before Palancia’s performance, saw it too.</p><p>“It was amazing,” Ballesteros, a 22-year-old from Venezuela, told the Sun-Times Wednesday. “Watching it, I felt all the emotions. It was a really good experience for the players and coaches. I feel happy it was a first for the country.”</p><p>Ballesteros sent a congratulatory text Wednesday morning as Palancia was preparing to fly from Miami to Phoenix. Disappointed team USA members Pete Crow-Armstong and Alex Bregman were also en route but not expected in camp until Friday, following Thursday’s off day. Saturday could be the first day any of them return to game action.</p><p>All of them have designs on a deep postseason, with goals of replicating “world champion” celebration snapshots like Palencia’s. So it didn’t hurt to have the guy who might be called on to get the last three outs of a monumental Cubs game this fall get the last three in one so majestic and historic for his country.</p><p>“He got a lot out of it,” Counsell said.</p><p>“It’s really a confidence thing for me. You’re in a really big moment, you know how you react in those moments. To have success in a moment like that gives you a lot of confidence.”</p><p>The Cubs had input into Palancia’s usage in the tournament as major league teams do, including the title game. Trying to balance the risk of overuse in March and allowing him the experience was the thing, and they signed off on a one-inning save situation. It was his second game in two days and third in four.</p><p>“The usage is more than anybody would like at this time of year,” Counsell said. “But the moment was also a moment you want someone to experience.”</p><p>After throwing 15 pitches in the semifinals against Italy Monday, Palencia came back for 13 pitches Tuesday. He struck out Kyle Schwarber with a 98.5 heater and got Gunnar Henderson to pop out before finishing off Anthony and the Americans.</p><p>USA manager Mark DeRosa respected the Padres’ request and did not use triple-digits velo closer Mason Miller, perhaps the best reliever in baseball, against Venezuela after Miller threw 22 pitches in the ninth inning of a 2-1 win over the Dominican Republic Sunday.</p><p>The Cubs were also OK with letting Palencia pitch in a save situation, even on consecutive days. The country of Venezuela thanks them.</p><p>“If it’s going to be usage that we’re comfortable with,” Counsell said, “we wanted to make sure it was in the moment that every player should get to experience.”</p><p>In five scoreless one-inning appearances in the WBC, Palencia did not allowing a hit, issued one walk and hit one batter and struck out nine. He was 3-for-3 in save opportunities.</p><p>“The experience was good for him,” Ballesteros said. “Physically, Palencia is ready. He worked hard, he worked a lot. He prepared for that.”<br></p>
entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen
